Title: Imidazolinone-tolerant crops: history, current status and future

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Thiamine pyrophosphate-dependent enzyme; catalytic large subunit (LSU) with α, β, γ domains
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Zea mays; Brassica napus; Oryza sativa; Triticum aestivum; Helianthus annuus
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Zea mays; Brassica napus; Oryza sativa; Triticum aestivum; Helianthus annuus
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: A188×B73; UE95; Topas; AS3510; Cypress; Fidel; Teal; wild sunflower near Rossville, Kansas
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Clearfield maize; Clearfield canola; CL121; CL141; CL161; XL8; Clearfield wheat; Clearfield sunflower
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential amino acids for plant growth and development
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Common upstream step for valine, leucine and isoleucine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the first common step in branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis, forming acetolactate or acetohydroxybutyrate from pyruvate-derived substrates
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms early committed intermediates required for branched-chain amino acid production
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Critical enzyme for branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis; herbicide target-site key control point
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Variant AHAS alleles reduce sensitivity to imidazolinone herbicides, enabling continued branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis and crop tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AHAS is the direct target of imidazolinone herbicides. 2. Single amino-acid substitutions in AHAS confer herbicide tolerance in multiple crops. 3. Commercial Clearfield crops mainly use Ser653Asn, Trp574Leu, Ala122Thr, Ala205Val, Ala155Thr, or Gly654Glu substitutions depending on species. 4. Stacking tolerant AHAS alleles can increase tolerance level.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutagenesis/selection; DNA sequencing of AHAS alleles; inheritance analysis; AHAS enzyme/herbicide sensitivity characterization; herbicide tolerance phenotyping in field and greenhouse
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Application of imidazolinone herbicides (imazapyr, imazapic, imazethapyr, imazamox, imazamethabenz, imazaquin); also other AHAS inhibitors for cross-tolerance testing
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: In oilseed rape, AHAS1 and AHAS3 are constitutively expressed and encode primary AHAS activities essential to growth and development
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Cross-tolerance may promote herbicide-resistant weed selection; gene flow from crop to related weeds; stewardship required
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Single target-site mutation can be introgressed by conventional breeding to generate non-transgenic herbicide-tolerant crops; allele stacking increases tolerance in some species
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review paper. The focal metabolism-related gene is AHAS/ALS across species rather than one single accession. In eukaryotes the enzyme likely contains catalytic and regulatory subunits. Tolerance trait is semi-dominant in commercial crops.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the first common step in branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: QJ22 mutation in als1 with Ser653Asn confers imidazolinone tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Maize contains two AHAS loci, als1 and als2. 2. QJ22 is believed to be a mutation of als1. 3. Ser653Asn in als1 confers tolerance to imidazolinones but not sulfonylureas.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic locus assignment; DNA sequencing; herbicide response profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful non-transgenic allele source for imidazolinone-tolerant maize breeding
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: QJ22 is believed to be als1 based on the review; locus assignment phrased cautiously.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: XI12; XA17; mutant 1; mutant 2 commercial maize derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the first common step in branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: XI12 and XA17 are allelic mutations of als2; Ser653Asn confers imidazolinone-only tolerance and Trp574Leu confers broad cross-tolerance; Ala122Thr also confers imidazolinone tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. XI12 and XA17 are allelic mutants of maize als2. 2. Ser653Asn in XI12 confers imidazolinone-specific tolerance. 3. Trp574Leu in XA17 confers tolerance to multiple AHAS inhibitor families. 4. Ala122Thr in mutant 1 also confers imidazolinone tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Allelic analysis; DNA sequencing; herbicide sensitivity profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Selection with imazaquin or EMS mutagenesis followed by imidazolinone treatment
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Cross-tolerance may affect herbicide rotation strategies
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Major breeding source of Clearfield maize tolerance alleles
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Maize has two reported AHAS loci with 95% homology.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the first common step in branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PM1 corresponds to AHAS1 with Ser653Asn, conferring imidazolinone tolerance only
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. B. napus has five AHAS loci. 2. AHAS1 is one of two constitutively expressed primary AHAS genes. 3. PM1 carries Ser653Asn in AHAS1 and confers imidazolinone-only tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutagenesis and selection; linkage/inheritance analysis; DNA sequencing
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Constitutively expressed; encodes primary AHAS activity essential to growth and development
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: PM1 alone gives lower tolerance than PM2
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful allele for stacking with AHAS3/PM2 to maximize canola tolerance

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the first common step in branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PM2 corresponds to AHAS3 with Trp574Leu, conferring stronger imidazolinone tolerance and cross-tolerance to sulfonylureas
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AHAS3 is constitutively expressed and essential in B. napus. 2. PM2 carries Trp574Leu in AHAS3. 3. PM2 gives higher imidazolinone tolerance than PM1. 4. Stacking PM1 and PM2 produces the highest tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutagenesis and selection; linkage/inheritance analysis; DNA sequencing; comparative herbicide tolerance phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Constitutively expressed; encodes primary AHAS activity essential to growth and development
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Cross-tolerance broadens selection pressure on weeds
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value allele for canola breeding; especially effective when stacked with PM1

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Regulation of AHAS catalytic activity in branched-chain amino acid biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Putative regulatory AHAS SSU forms the regulatory subunit of the AHAS enzyme in eukaryotes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Eukaryotic AHAS is suggested to comprise a catalytic subunit and a regulatory small subunit. 2. Putative AHAS SSUs have been cloned and expressed in several plant species. 3. The review does not assign a specific commercial tolerance trait to SSU.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning/expression in several plant species; structural and biochemical inference
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Contributes to AHAS holoenzyme organization but not identified as the commercial tolerance determinant in this review
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Forms AHAS holoenzyme regulatory subunit with catalytic LSU dimer
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential amino acids for plant growth and development
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as background/mechanistic context rather than a commercial core tolerance gene; included because the review explicitly discusses its regulatory role in AHAS enzyme architecture.
